Title: Toward fair medical advice: Addressing and mitigating bias in large language model-based healthcare applications.
Paper Type: Dataset or Benchmark, Experiment on Existing Models
Data Source: MedQA, synthetic patient data generated using Synthea, and private data set from the Commonwealth Bank Health Society (CBHS)
Data Details: Each data point is either a question and answer pair or a patient record, either a de-indentified record from the real world or a synthetically generated one. The MedQA dataset includes 383,093 questions across its training, development, and test sets. However, only a subset contains patient demographic information. For this study, we used this subset, which consists of 226 patients (119 males and 107 females). Synthea is an open-source tool that generates synthetic healthcare records, and the researchers used it to generate 305 different records. The CBHS dataset comprises de-identified administrative claims data from 123,983 patients, and researchers used a subset of 528 patients.
Topic: Researchers developed FairMED, a framework to mitigate bias of healthcare LLMs and improve fairness and impartiality. Researchers analyze sentiment and use of directive language in the model's output. They also fine-tune a model that is already specialized to the medical domain to improve outcomes.
Notes: Researchers used like "SentimentIntensityAnalyzer from the VADER library", directive language classification. They use statistical parity difference, disparate impact ratio, and kullback–leibler divergence as language-based fairness metrics. As for content-level fairness metrics, they use diagnostic recommendation divergence, action disparity index, referral frequency parity to evaluate textual LLM output. More directive language is seen as positive, as it improves clarity of communication. Across the three datasets used, the trend was that female patients received more positive sentiment while males received more directive language.
Debias Details: Researchers fine-tune Llama3-Med42-8B model on 400 different question answer pairs using the QLoRA technique. Researchers used different inference approaches, which they refer to as Zero-Shot FairMed (default zero shot methods), FairMed - without (remove sensitive attributes), FairMed - Demographic-Neutral Prompting (generalizes reponses across groups), FairMed - Counterfactual Prompting (considers alternative demographic scenarios), FairMed - Descriptive Prompting with Fairness Criteria (explicitly utilizes fairness principles)

Title: Through a Glass Darkly: Perceptions of Ethnoracial Identity in Artificial Intelligence Generated Medical Vignettes and Images.
Data Source: Originally written prompts by the researchers
Data Details: This paper does not use a dataset in the sense of testing an LLM on questions or input collected from a dataset. Instead, researchers wrote a small number of prompts, with a variable in the prompt being what condition or disease the prompt was about. Thus, each data point is a originally written prompt for the LLMs with some blanks that would be filled. For ChatGPT, researchers used a three-step prompting method, and for the Microsoft Bing Image Creator, they used a two-step prompting method. This adds up to 5 different data points.
Topic: Researchers investigate the potential for AI image generators to perpetuate ethnoracial stereotypes in medical cases through prompting and observing demographic information outputed.
Notes: Bias stems from training data and user-dependent prompting. Self-prompting (asking AI to re-use its own output) sometimes intensified stereotypes, but never really accurate to real life percentages.
Racial Bias: Yes(African, Mediterranean, Caucasian, Ashkenazi Jewish, and East Asian)
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT, Microsoft’s Bing A.I. Image Creator

Title: Mixed methods assessment of the influence of demographics on medical advice of ChatGPT.
Data Source: WebMD symptom checker
Data Details: This paper does not use a dataset in the sense of testing an LLM on questions or input collected from a dataset. Researchers wrote their own template for prompts and they applied that to different demographic combinations. In the end, they had 540 prompts. Hence, each data point is a prompt for the LLM written with certain medical and demographic information.
Topic: Researchers evaluated demographic biases in diagnostic accuracy and health advice between generative artificial intelligence (AI) (ChatGPT GPT-4) and traditional symptom checkers like WebMD.
Notes: ChatGPT matched WebMD in 91% of diagnoses, with a 24% top diagnosis match rate. Diagnostic accuracy was not significantly different across demographic groups, including age, race/ethnicity, and sex.

Title: Assessing the potential of GPT-4 to perpetuate racial and gender biases in health care: a model evaluation study.
Data Source: NEJM Healer, originally written prompts
Data Details: Each data point is a written prompt that is matched with 18 different possible disease diagnoses, which makes 180 different prompt-demographic pairs. Researchers wrote 10 different prompts that request the LLM to output a type of clinical presentation. They ran each of these data points 100 times through ChatGPT-4. Additionally, they also used 19 medical educational cases from NEJM Healer. With some modification of the original data, researchers formatted each data point to include the top 10 most likely diagnoses in descending order, a list of life threatening diagnoses, next steps, and treatment steps.
Topic: Researchers tested whether GPT-4 encodes racial and gender biases and examined the impact of such biases on four potential applications of LLMs in the clinical domain, namely, medical education, diagnostic reasoning, clinical plan generation, and subjective patient assessment.
Notes: Researchers used multivariate logistic regression and pairwise Mann-Whitney tests to assess differences in diagnosis rank across demographic groups.
Debias Details: "We further assessed whether including the geographic setting or using debiasing strategies would affect GPT-4's estimations of disease prevalence by demographic group... The two prompt debiasing strategies produced variable results,"

Title: Sociodemographic biases in medical decision making by large language models.
Data Source: 500 real cases sampled from the Mount Sinai Health System, as well as 500 synthetically generated cases.
Data Details: Each data point is an emergency department case and a structured clinical vignette.
Topic: Compare LLM model outputs by demographic information with real data and with control group.
Notes: "across nine LLMs and 32 sociodemographic groups, shows that LLM-generated clinical recommendations often vary with case demographics rather than with clinical factors alone... We compared model outputs to a physician-derived reference and to the model’s own control (no sociodemographic descriptor) to detect possible variations in recommendations... assessed which sociodemographic case groups had the highest invasiveness scores compared to the control group". The ground truth was established as board certified physicians independently answered questions given the vignettes.

Title: Racial, ethnic, and sex bias in large language model opioid recommendations for pain management.
Data Source: MIMIC-IV Note
Data Details: Each data point is a real and deidentified clinical case.
Topic: This exploratory investigation uses real, deidentified clinical cases to investigate how GPT-4 and Gemini ratings of pain severity and recommendations for treatment with opioid medication vary based on patient race, ethnicity, and sex.
Notes: "No significant differences in opioid recommendations were found across patient racial/ethnic group or sex... these results point towards a potential of incorporating LLMs into pain management"
Racial Bias: Yes (American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Black, Hispanic or Latino, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, White)

Title: Evaluating anti-LGBTQIA+ medical bias in large language models.
Data Source: Researchers consulted medical experts in LGBTQIA+ health and created prompts consisting of explicit questions and synthetic clinical notes.
Data Details: The data was written by medical students and clinicians specialized in LGBTQIA+ health. Each data point is either an explicit question or a clinical scenario that can illuminate anti-LGBTQIA+ bias.
Topic: Researchers prompted LLMs and graded outputs with a primary reviewer based on criteria outlined in conjunction with LGBTQIA+ health experts. They asked questions where historical bias has and has not been observed and also considered instances where one's LGBTQIA identity should affect medical output.
Notes: Any level of propagation of bias (e.g., racial, socioeconomic, gender-related), defined as inaccurate and/or stereotyped statements, with a focus on anti-LGBTQIA+ bias. This was marked inappropriate if there were explicit statements (e.g., phalloplasty will affect eGFR) or if it could be inferred implicitly from the response, especially in comparison to the response for the paired prompt that does not mention a LGBTQIA+ identity.
Evaluated LLMs: Gemini 1.5 Flash, Claude 3 Haiku, GPT-4o, Stanford Medicine Secure GPT

Title: A toolbox for surfacing health equity harms and biases in large language models.
Data Source: Researchers used non-adversarial HealthSearchQA and MultiMedQA datasets from another study. They use 9 questions from the study "Large language models propagate race-based medicine". They also use datasets called MultiMedQA and Open-ended Medical Adversarial Queries.
Data Details: Researchers created a new dataset called EquityMedQA that itself consists of 7 datasets. Note that each different dataset studies different types of demographic bias, although the different groups studied is not specified. Each data point is an adversarial question intended to enable evaluation of biases.
Topic: Evaluating LLM model bias using six definitional dimensions of bias: covering inaccuracy across axes of identity, lack of inclusion, stereotypical language or characterization, omission of structural explanations for inequity, failure to challenge a biased premise and potential for disproportionate withholding of opportunities or resources
Notes: We used the three assessment rubrics described previously (independent, pairwise and counterfactual) on answers to questions from each of the datasets.

Title: Large language model comparisons between English and Chinese query performance for cardiovascular prevention.
Data Source: Written by cardiologists recruited for this study.
Data Details: Experienced cardiologists created a dataset of 300 questions covering domains such as biomarker, medication information, dyslipidemia, hypertension, diet counseling, diabetes mellitus and/or chronic kidney disease, secondary prevention, prevention strategy, inflammation, exercise counseling, obesity, and tobacco treatment. We then translated these questions into Chinese. Each data point is a question either in Chinese or English.
Topic: Researchers evaluated the ability of English and Chinese LLMs to answer questions related to cardiovascular disease prevention.
Notes: "LLM-chatbot performed generally better with English prompts than with Chinese prompts."

Title: ChatGPT's ability to comprehend and answer cirrhosis related questions in Arabic.
Data Source: 91 questions in Arabic and English
Data Details: "Questions were also collected from Facebook support groups created for patients with cirrhosis." Each data point is a different question.
Topic: ChatGPTs responses to 91 questions in Arabic and English were graded by a transplant hepatologist fluent in both languages.
Notes: Accuracy of responses was assessed using the scale: 1. Comprehensive, 2. Correct but inadequate, 3. Mixed with correct and incorrect/outdated data, and 4. Completely incorrect. Accuracy of Arabic compared to English responses was assessed using the scale: 1. Arabic response is more accurate, 2. Similar accuracy, 3. Arabic response is less accurate

Title: Gender Bias in AI's Perception of Cardiovascular Risk.
Data Source: Generated Data
Data Details: We generated hypothetical clinical scenarios by matching men and women with identical chest pain types (typical, atypical, and nonanginal) using the same symptom descriptions.
Topic: Researchers measured the effect of gender description in prompts on evaluation of risk given cardiovascular symptoms.
Notes: Researchers generated hypothetical clinical scenarios and inputted them to ChatGPT-4. There were two descriptions, and the only difference was that the patient was either a man or woman, but the symptoms where the same: identical chest pain types (typical, atypical, and nonanginal) using the same descriptions. They asked the model to select which patient was at higher risk. Potential interpretations of the study include mental health stigma and undervaluing the symptom reporting of women.

Title: Gender and Ethnicity Bias of Text-to-Image Generative Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging, Part 1: Preliminary Evaluation.
Data Source: Originally Written
Data Details: Researchers prompted models to generate pictures of medical professionals in a medical subfield. Each data point is a prompt.
Topic: Use image generation models to generate a series of individual and group images of medical imaging professionals, and analyze their race and gender characteristics.
Notes: Multiple iterations of images were generated using a variety of prompts. Collectively, 184 images were produced for evaluation of 391 characters. All images were independently analyzed by 3 reviewers for apparent gender and skin tone.
Evaluated LLMs: DALL-E 3, Firefly 2, Stable Diffusion 2.1, and Midjourney 5.2

Title: Bias and Inaccuracy in AI Chatbot Ophthalmologist Recommendations.
Data Source: Generated Data
Data Details: Each Chatbot was asked the following prompt, "Find me four good ophthalmologists in (city)". This was asked for the 20 most populus cities in the US at the time of the study. Each data point is defined as a different prompt given to a single chatbot.
Topic: Researchers evaluated gender bias in ophthalmologist recommendations from well known chatbots.
Notes: The LLMs struggled to recommend ophthalmologists reliably and accurately, with most recommendations being physicians in specialties other than ophthalmology, deceased, or not in or near the desired city. There was also a bias towards academic medicine. Researchers observed a statistically significant difference between the proportion of recommended female ophthalmologists and the national proportion of practicing female ophthalmologists, in that female ophthalmologists were underrepresented in the chatbot recommendations.

Title: Assessing Biases in Medical Decisions via Clinician and AI Chatbot Responses to Patient Vignettes.
Data Source:
The data is pulled from 8 other existing papers.
1. Green AR, Carney DR, Pallin DJ, et al. Implicit Bias among Physicians and its Prediction of Thrombolysis Decisions for Black and White
Patients. J Gen Intern Med. 2007;22(9):1231. doi:10.1007/S11606-007-0258-5
2. Breathett K, Yee E, Pool N, et al. Association of Gender and Race With Allocation of Advanced Heart Failure Therapies + Editorial +
Supplemental content. JAMA Netw Open. 2020;3(7):2011044. doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2020.11044
3. Daugherty SL, Blair I V., Havranek EP, et al. Implicit gender bias and the use of cardiovascular tests among cardiologists. J Am Heart Assoc.
2017;6(12). doi:10.1161/JAHA.117.006872
4. Lutfey KE, Link CL, Marceau LD, et al. Diagnostic certainty as a source of medical practice variation in coronary heart disease: Results from
a cross-national experiment of clinical decision making. Med Decis Making. 2009;29(5):606. doi:10.1177/0272989X09331811
5. Centola D, Guilbeault D, Sarkar U, Khoong E, Zhang J. The reduction of race and gender bias in clinical treatment recommendations using
clinician peer networks in an experimental setting. Nat Commun 2021 121. 2021;12(1):1-10. doi:10.1038/s41467-021-26905-5
6. Haider AH, Sexton J, Sriram N, et al. Association of unconscious race and social class bias with vignette-based clinical assessments by
medical students. JAMA. 2011;306(9):942-951. doi:10.1001/JAMA.2011.1248
7. Simard JF, Chaichian Y, Rizk N, Rector A, Feldman CH, Falasinnu TO. ARE WE MISSING LUPUS IN MALES? EVIDENCE OF COGNITIVE
BIAS FROM A RANDOMIZED EXPERIMENT IN THE UNITED STATES. Am J Epidemiol. 2022;191(1):230. doi:10.1093/AJE/KWAB199
8. Zadu A, van Egmond S, de Vere Hunt I, Petion E, Cai Z, Yeung H, Simard J, Linos E. Prescribing Patterns for Acne in
 Transgender Compared to Cisgender Patients. Poster presented at American Academy of Dermatology 2023 Annual Meeting.
 March 17th, 2023; New Orleans, LA, USA.
Data Details: Researchers selected 19 clinical vignettes from literature across different disciplines like cardiology, emergency medicine, rheumatology, and dermatology. Each data point is a clinical vignette from existing literature. The cited sources can be found in their supplementary materials page, which includes 8 different sources.
Topic: Researchers evaluated the bias of LLM responses across many different demographic factors in different medical subfields.
Notes: Different LLM prompts had different outputs, with some on the Likert scale and others being yes or no. Regardless, researchers examined the output and looked for differences in responses.

Title: AI-driven translations for kidney transplant equity in Hispanic populations.
Data Source: Frequently asked questions from Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN), National Health Service (NHS), and National Kidney Foundation (NKF).
Data Details: Researchers collected frequently asked questions, or FAQs, from different sources. Firstly there were 19 questions focusing on eligibility criteria, waitlist process, and post-transplant care from Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN). Secondly, there were 5 questions focusing on patient preparation for kidney transplantation, surgical procedures, and post-transplant care from the National Health Service (NHS). Thirdly, there were 20 questions focusing on long-term management, lifestyle consideration, and support resources for kidney transplant recipients from the National Kidney Foundation (NKF). Each data point is a frequently asked question.
Topic: Researchers assess the accuracy of frequently asked questions on kidney health both in linguistic accuracy and cultural sensitivity.
Notes: The models' translations where assessed by Mexican native Spanish speaking nephrologists using a rubric with values from 1 to 5. The translates were assessed on linguistic accuracy, which includes "grammatical correctness, appropriate use of vocabulary, and syntactic integrity of the translations, clarity, readability, and technical precision in medical terminology". It was also assessed on cultural sensitivity, which includes "the extent to which translations respected and incorporated cultural nuances, idiomatic expressions, and contextually relevant information for the Hispanic community."

Title: Physician clinical decision modification and bias assessment in a randomized controlled trial of AI assistance.
Data Source: There are 2 videos of a standard patient actor depicting a case of chest pain in an outpatient setting, with the actor being either a white male or a black female. 50 physicians are invited to view these videos.
Data Details: Each data point is a physician or resident physicians with training in a general medical specialty (internal medicine, family medicine, or emergency medicine) viewing one of two videos.
Topic: Researchers studied how AI assistance affect physician accuracy or bias.
Notes: Summary from the paper: "We investigated how doctors respond to AI suggestions when evaluating chest pain, a common but serious medical concern. We showed 50 doctors a video of either a white male or Black female patient describing chest pain symptoms, and asked them to make medical decisions. The doctors then received suggestions from an AI system and could change their decisions. We found that doctors were willing to consider the AI’s suggestions and made more accurate medical decisions after receiving this help. This improvement in decision-making happened equally for all patients, regardless of their race or gender, suggesting AI tools could help improve medical care without increasing bias."

Title: Ensuring Accuracy and Equity in Vaccination Information From ChatGPT and CDC: Mixed-Methods Cross-Language Evaluation.
Data Source: CDC website in both English and Spanish
Data Details: Each datapoint is a frequently asked vaccination-related question on the CDC website, either in English or Spanish. There are 16 questions in each language.
Topic: Researchers measured accuracy, understandability, and readability of ChatGPT's responses to frequently asked vaccination-related questions on the CDC website
Notes: There were human readers who assessed the misinformation levels and readability, but also objective system-based metrics for readability, like the Flesch-Kincaid grade level score.

Title: Exploring Biases of Large Language Models in the Field of Mental Health: Comparative Questionnaire Study of the Effect of Gender and Sexual Orientation in Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia Nervosa Case Vignettes.
Data Source: 12 different scientific papers from PubMed and Google Scholar.
Data Details: In the existing scientific papers, researchers found 30 vignettes of patients with Anorexia Nervosa or Bulimia Nervosa. They then adapted gender and sexual orientation in each vignette to create 4 version of each one, resulting in 120 different vignettes to input to LLMs.
Topic: Researchers prompted ChatGPT-4 and MentaLLaMAto score measures of mental health-related quality of life given prompts with different gender and sexual orientations.
Notes: ChatGPT-4 showed a gender bias. It rated men as having poorer mental quality of life than equivalent women with the same symptoms. No bias was found for sexual orientation. There are two metrics, like the SF-36 metric that assesses HRQoL and consists of subscales like physical functioning, bodily pain, etc. Also, the eating disorder examination questionnaire (EDE-Q) assesses ED symptomatology, including subscales like dietary restraint and weight concern.

Title: Evaluating for Evidence of Sociodemographic Bias in Conversational AI for Mental Health Support.
Data Source: Generated Prompts
Data Details: The researchers used ChatGPT-3.5 to generate patients' complaints and subsequent responses to a conversational agent. Had ChatGPT-3.5 have a conversation between the agent, and they tried this with 97 distinct demographic combinations. They tested each combination 4 or 5 times, which means researchers recorded 449 conversation transcripts. Each datapoint is a distinct demographic combination to give to ChatGPT to hold a conversation with an agent.
Topic: In this study, we present an approach to investigate the presence of bias within an LLM designed for mental health support.
Notes: We simulated physician–patient conversations by using a communication loop between an LLM-based conversational agent and digital standardized patients (DSPs) that engaged the agent in dialogue while remaining agnostic to sociodemographic characteristics. The metric for bias is LIWC, a text analysis software that quantifies psychological and linguistic components in text.
Evaluated LLMs: LLM-based program previously developed and tested at Cedars-Sinai Medical Center

Title: Fairness in AI-Driven Oncology: Investigating Racial and Gender Biases in Large Language Models.
Data Source: Generated Prompts
Data Details: Researchers prompted LLMs with racial and gender demographic information of a healthcare professional in the oncology field. Then they asked it on a numerical scale from 1 to 5 of what kind of roles they would be doing, with one being a nurse and 5 being a doctor. They also asked for oncologist recommendations in certain cities, among other questions. Each data point in this case is a different prompt the researchers wrote.
Topic: Researchers prompted LLMs with questions related to the roles of oncology professionals based on demographic data.
Notes: They compared the outputs of the LLMs to actual healthcare statistics on the demographics of healthcare professionals as well as patient demographics for certain diseases, for the other questions related to certain diseases.

Title: Mitigating the risk of health inequity exacerbated by large language models.
Data Source: They used five datasets. SIGIR 2016 (Proceedings of the 39th International ACM SIGIR conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval), TREC 2011, and TREC 2022 (Clinical Trial datasets), while the medical question answering, or MQA, datasets are MedQA8 and MedMCQA
Data Details: Researchers developed the EquityGuard framework to detect and mitigate bias in LLM outputs.
Topic: Researchers examined how including socio-demographic factors leads LLMs to biased/mistaken outputs, and proposes mitigation.
Notes: Used metric called Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 10. This evaluated performance on tasks related to clinical trial matching and medical question answering.
Racial Bias: Yes(african american,asian,black,east asian,hispanic,indigenous,middle eastern,native american,mixed race,pacfic islander,south asian,white)

Title: Invisible Bias in GPT-4o-mini: Detecting Disparities in AI-Generated Patient Messaging.
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: Data generated by ChatGPT-4o-mini
Topic: Researchers used an LLM to score LLM-generated patient conversations on empathy, encouragement, accuracy, clarity, professionalism, and respect.
Notes: The study also studies demographic divisions along the lines of housing status, including categories like homeless, living alone, living with family, living in a care facility, or living in a shelter. It also studied religious demographics, as well as different marital statuses.
Racial Bias: Yes(black,east asian,hispanic or latino,middle eastern,other,south asian,white)

Title: Benchmarking bias in embeddings of healthcare AI models: using SD-WEAT for detection and measurement across sensitive populations.
Journal: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Other studies
Data Details: Referred to in the study as G-1,G-2,G-3,G-4
Topic: Researchers studied ways to measure bias in LLMs using word embeddings methods.
Notes: They examined hereditary conditions on small populations known to have certain diseases, namely Finnish and Ashkenazi Jewish.

Title: Assessing Racial and Ethnic Bias in Text Generation by Large Language Models for Health Care-Related Tasks: Cross-Sectional Study.
Data Source: Used structured hospital encounter metadata in a table format from 100 randomly selected fully deidentified encounters for patients with HIV.
Data Details: Each data point is includes the patient’s demographics, primary encounter diagnosis, and HIV disease control status at the time of the encounters.
Topic: Researchers prompted LLMs to write discharge instructions for patients with HIV and analyzed the responses for sentiment, subjectivity, reading ease, and word frequency by race/ethnicity given in the prompt.
Notes: Yes (racial/ethnic)

Title: Potential to perpetuate social biases in health care by Chinese large language models: a model evaluation study.
Journal: International Journal for Equity in Health
Data Source:
Used 19 clinical cases proposed by the New England Journal of
Medicine (NEJM) Healer, which helped create 32 prompts for each
clinical vignette, representing varied combinations of the
patient sociodemographic characteristics. Also used 8 clinical vignettes 
developed by another researcher for patients' needs assessment. 
They made 32 prompts from that too. They also generated some 
synthetic data from the LMs for 3 select diseases.
Data Details: Each datapoint is a clinical vignette from NEJM healer.
Topic: Researchers tested how much Chinese LLMs perpetuated bias in patient need assessment, differential diagnosis and treatment planning.

Title: Evaluating the Performance and Bias of Natural Language Processing Tools in Labeling Chest Radiograph Reports.
Data Source:
This study uses chest radiograph (CXR) report from an Indiana University dataset and chest radiograph reports from the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care, or MIMIC. 
(MIMIC) dataset.
Data Details: Each data point is a chest radiograph, with 3665 from the Indiana University Dataset and 692 from the MIMIC dataset. Evaluation of NLP tools for labeling radiograph reports & bias assessment
Topic: Researchers compared the labeling of chest radiograph reports of LLMs and other NLP models compared to radiologists.
Notes: Labels include 14 different diseases.

Title: Identifying social determinants of health from clinical narratives: A study of performance, documentation ratio, and potential bias.
Data Source: UF Health Integrated Data Repository
Data Details: This study used clinical narratives from UF Health Integrated Data Repository (IDR). The UF Health IDR is a clinical data warehouse that aggregates data from the university’s various clinical and administrative information systems, including the Epic (Epic Systems Corporation) system.
Topic: Compared seven transformer-based large language models (including BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa, Longformer, and GatorTron) for extracting SDoH concepts and linking their attributes.
Notes: Researchers developed an SDoH corpus.
Debias Details: The cancer SDoH corpus was used to train, validate, and test transformer-based models: BERT, RoBERTa, GatorTron, DeBERTa, Longformer

Title: How Can We Diagnose and Treat Bias in Large Language Models for Clinical Decision-Making?
Journal: Proceedings of the 2025 Conference of the Nations of the Americas Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies (Volume 1: Long Papers)
Data Source: JAMA Clinical Challenge, two dataset: JAMA_Chen2024 dataset and JAMA_CPV dataset
Data Details: Each case comprises a detailed patient description (250 words), a specific clinical question, four answer options, the correct answer index, a discussion (500-600 words) elaborating on the preferred option, and a medical speciality classification. Each data point is a challenge question with description for the LLMs.
Topic: Alternate dataset and evaluation techniques for debiasing.
Notes: “We introduce a novel Counterfactual Patient Variations (CPV) dataset derived from the JAMA Clinical Challenge... By combining accuracy comparisons, statistical methods, SHAP analysis, and embedding-based measures, we provide a holistic view of bias manifestation, offering insights into performance disparities, underlying model behaviours, and latent biases in language representations.”
Debias Details: "We developed multiple prompting strategies to evaluate different approaches to bias mitigation... we fine-tuned models on a dataset with case descriptions and options"

Title: Unveiling Performance Challenges of Large Language Models in Low-Resource Healthcare: A Demographic Fairness Perspective
Journal: Proceedings of the 31st International Conference on Computational Linguistics
Data Source: MEDQA,MIMIC-IV
Data Details: Some datasets contained data on contains transcribed interactions between a trained clinician and outpatients with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and healthy controls. Others contained multiple-choice medical problems collected from medical board exams
Topic: Evaluating bias in performance of Healthcare LLMs
Notes: Explicitly prompting LLMs with demographic information does not necessarily improve performance. The impact varies depending on both the specific task and the LLM used.
Debias Details: Researchers tried different prompting techniques, like Zero-Shot Chain of Thought, N-shot CoT/

Title: Gender-Neutral Large Language Models for Medical Applications: Reducing Bias in PubMed Abstracts
Journal: Proceedings of the 24th Workshop on Biomedical Language Processing
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Experiment on New Models
Data Source: 379,000 PubMed abstracts
Data Details: Each data point is a abstract from PubMed's database of papers.
Topic: Propose a novel approach to mitigate gender bias in LLMs used in medical contexts.
Notes: In each sentence, a [MASK] token was inserted in place of a pronoun, and the model was tasked with predicting the correct pronoun when given respective options of ‘he/him/his,’ ‘she/her/hers,’ and ‘they/them/theirs.’.... Augmented datasets improved both accuracy and fairness in classification tasks.
Debias Details: Researchers neutered the gendered pronouns in abstracts and trained a model on the new data.

Title: Unlocking LLMs: Addressing Scarce Data and Bias Challenges in Mental Health and Therapeutic Counselling
Journal: International Conference on Natural Language Processing and Artificial Intelligence for Cyber Security
Data Source: AnnoMI
Data Details: an expert-annotated GDPR-compliant dataset of 133 high- and low-quality MI sessions
Topic: Researchers used an existing dataset and ChatGPT to write synthetic data with human experts in the loop to create a larger and more balanced dataset called IC-AnnoMI for mental health LLMs.
Notes: To maintain the integrity and quality of the data, qualified expert affiliated with the gold-standard organisation in psychology and MI have performed the annotations.
Debias Details: They used a prompting technique called progressive prompting. Made the data more diverse and balanced. Kept the quality high by having humans in the loop

Title: DiversityMedQA: A Benchmark for Assessing Demographic Biases in Medical Diagnosis using Large Language Models
Journal: Proceedings of the Third Workshop on NLP for Positive Impact
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: MedQA
Data Details:
MedQA is a standardized collection
of medical questions from professional medical
board exams. Each data point is a medical question.
Topic: We introduce DiversityMedQA1 , a novel benchmark designed to assess LLM responses to medical queries across diverse patient demographics, such as gender and ethnicity
Notes: Most interesting finding: “After qualitative analysis, we noticed that certain words triggered differences in how the models answered questions. Words that related to emotions and actions of distress, such as "crying" or "clutching abdomen", lead to questions being answered differently depending on gender. This behavior is especially pervasive in the GPT3.5 and Llama3-8B models, but only has a minor yet noteworthy presence in the other models. This is likely happening due to the models perpetuating stereotypical differences in how different genders experience pain.”
Evaluated LLMs: GPT-3.5 Turbo, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Flash, and Llama3-8B

Title: Can LLMs Replace Clinical Doctors? Exploring Bias in Disease Diagnosis by Large Language Models
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2024
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: 336,920 authentic medical records from a hospital
Data Details: Each data point is a medical record from a hospital.
Topic: LLMs Show Systematic Biases in Diagnosis.
Notes: Types of Bias: Gender Bias: GPT-4 and Qwen favored male diagnoses; ChatGPT favored female ones. Over-reliance on gender or age in reasoning led to > 98% error rates (page 7, Figure 7). Age Bias: All models biased toward middle-aged patients, reflecting training-data imbalance.
Debias Details: Reseachers propose a technique which they call the Integrated Debiasing Diagnosis Method, which first makes the model provide output when not given any demographic information.

Title: PersonaTwin: A Multi-Tier Prompt Conditioning Framework for Generating and Evaluating Personalized Digital Twins
Journal: Proceedings of the Fourth Workshop on Generation, Evaluation and Metrics (GEM²)
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source:
Used a large-scale psychometric dataset of more than 8,500 respondents from a previous study: "Constructing
a psychometric testbed for fair natural language processing."
Data Details: Each data points is a combination of survey-based measures, user-generated text, and demographic information, including responses on health numeracy, medical visit anxiety, and trust in healthcare providers.
Topic: Analyzes how LLMs introduce bias in sexual and reproductive health responses
Notes: LLMs favored young adult females and specific geographic regions
Racial Bias:
Yes(white,black or african american,asian,native american or american indian,
native hawaiian or Pacific Islander, multiracial or biracial, other)
Other Bias: Yes(no_college,higher education, range of income <$20,000 to >$90,000)
Debias Details: Using different prompting techniques, researchers recorded metrics like MSE, Pearson’s R, F1, AUC, and Disparity Index for different demographic cleavages like age, race, gender, income, and education.

Title: AraTrust: An Evaluation of Trustworthiness for LLMs in Arabic
Journal: Proceedings of the 31st International Conference on Computational Linguistics
Data Source: Human-written multiple‐choice benchmark (Arabic)
Data Details: Each data point is a written multiple-choice questions spanning truthfulness, ethics, safety, health, unfairness etc. Researchers drew from authentic, human-generated sources, like colleague exams. We also used on existing datasets and online websites, like 18 samples from Arabic Hate Speech, a toxicity detection dataset curated from Twitter, and modified them to create questions assessing LLMs’ ability to detect offensive and toxic language, and they wrote 522 data points.
Topic:
Researchers develop AraTrust, a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate Arabic LLMs’
trustworthiness with multiple-choice questions. They focus on the following categories of trustworthiness: truthfulness, ethics, physical health, mental health, unfairness, illegal activities, privacy, and offensive language.
Notes: They found differences in trustworthiness and performance between the large proprietary models and the Arabic specific language model.
Debias Details: Researchers used zero-shot, one-shot, few-shot, zero-shot CoT prompting methods to evalute the trustworthiness of the LLMs. They did some preliminary, although less formal and not recorded, and they found that Arabic prompts generate more accurate responses than English prompts.

Title: AfriMed-QA: A Pan-African, Multi-Specialty, Medical Question-Answering Benchmark Dataset
Journal: Proceedings of the 63rd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
Data Source: New data source.
Data Details:
New data source. Medical trainees and clinicians
were recruited to contribute questions through referrals.
Topic: Evaluating LLM performance and bias in African medical contexts
Notes: Biomedical LLMs underperform; significant regional and specialty variation

Title: Can AI Relate: Testing Large Language Model Response for Mental Health Support
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2024
Data Source: LLM responses to user posts in mental health forums (e.g., Reddit)
Data Details: Each data point is either a post or a peer response from Reddit. Framework measuring empathy and motivational interviewing adherence; human evaluators compared LLM vs peer support responses.
Topic: Examines empathy, equity, and quality of LLM responses in mental health support
Notes: LLMs showed lower empathy (2–13%) toward Black posters; disparities in emotional alignment
Debias Details: Shows how generation methods affect empathy; suggests ethical and safety guidelines

Title: Evaluating Biases in Context-Dependent Sexual and Reproductive Health Questions
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2024
Data Source: Sexual and reproductive health QA dataset with demographic context (age, sex, location)
Data Details: Curated from Planned Parenthood and public health QA sites; tested with and without demographic context
Topic: Analyzes how LLMs introduce bias in sexual and reproductive health responses
Notes: LLMs favored young adult females and specific geographic regions

Title: Open (Clinical) LLMs are Sensitive to Instruction Phrasings
Journal: Proceedings of the 23rd Workshop on Biomedical Natural Language Processing
Data Source: MIMIC-III,Obesity co-morbidity,Cohort Selection,Medical Challenge,Relations Challenge,Adverse Drug Effects,Risk Assessment
Data Country:
United States, Nigeria, Kenya, Canada, Zambia,
Egypt, Malawi, Pakistan, Philippines, and Ethiopia.
Data Availability: Open Source, Needs a deeper dive
Data Details: Data comes in the form of different classification tasks and information extraction tasks from various different databases. Then, medical professionals from different countries were recruited to write prompts to instruct existing models to try and answer these questions.
Topic: Testing the ability of LLMs to answer medical challenges given natural variation in the wording of prompts written by actual medical professionals from different countries.
Notes: Since many of the tasks or challenges are classification, the study uses F1 or AUROC as a metric.

Title: ChiMed-GPT: A Chinese Medical Large Language Model with Full Training Regime and Better Alignment to Human Preferences
Journal: Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
Data Source:
Chinese Medical Dataset (CMD), 
Data all mentioned here on hugging face: https://huggingface.co/datasets/shibing624/medical#:~:text=%E5%8E%9F%E5%A7%8B%E6%95%B0%E6%8D%AE%E9%9B%86%EF%BC%9A-,google%20drive,-%EF%BC%8C%E5%8F%AA%E5%AF%B9%E9%95%BF
Sublinks:
Freedom Intelligence: 
https://huggingface.co/datasets/FreedomIntelligence/huatuo_encyclopedia_qa
Chinese Medical book:
https://drive.google.com/u/0/uc?export=download&confirm=t&id=1ImYUSLk9JbgHXOemfvyiDiirluZHPeQw
Chinese medical dialogue data: 
https://github.com/Toyhom/Chinese-medical-dialogue-data
English dialogue data, “Chatdoctor”:
https://github.com/Kent0n-Li/ChatDoctor
Chinese dialogue data for reward part.
https://github.com/Toyhom/Chinese-medical-dialogue-data
Other part of Chinese dialogue data:
https://github.com/SCIR-HI/Huatuo-Llama-Med-Chinese
Data Details: Mostly Chinese, but around a small portion of the data is in English. The data is broken up in to pretraining, fine-tuning, and reward data.
Topic: Developing a new CHIMED-GPT model fine-tuned on medical data and testing it based on a metric designed to measure doctors' attitudes towards patients with mental disorders and diseases.
Notes: They also evaluated the model in its performance in answering medical questions compared to other LLMs like ChatGPT, Ziya, Baichuan, Taiyi, and MedicalGPT. They test bias using the metric of the CAMI scale, which aims to measure public attitudes towards individuals suffering from mental disorders. They also use the MICA scale evaluates doctors’ attitudes towards patients with mental diseases through 16 statements.
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT, Ziya, Baichuan, Taiyi, and MedicalGPT. ChiMed-GPT

Title: Empirical Study of Social Bias in Medical Question Answering via Large Language Models
Journal: Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Conference Paper
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: College Medicine, Medical Genetics, Professional Medicine datasets from the MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) datasets.
Data Details: This dataset consists of multichoice questions covering many different branches of knowledge. Each data point is a multiple choice question.
Topic: Researchers prompted different LLMs with medical questions and demographic information about race, gender, and professional experience. They asked the LLM to answer the question as if it were someone in those demographic groups. They analyzed performance disparity between the different types of prompts.
Notes: Certain types of questions resulted in differences in performance between gender or racial groups. While gender gaps were typically small, the male answers were 3% more accurate in the professional medicine questions. While the racial gaps were not too significant, the Gemma model  saw 4% higher white performance than black on college medicine questions. However, they also saw higher scores often seen for Black prompts in college medicine questions for other models.
Other Bias: Professional Bias (First Year, Second Year, Preclinical, Clinical)

Title: Exposing and Mitigating Calibration Biases and Demographic Unfairness in MLLM Few-Shot In-Context Learning for Medical Image Classification
Journal: Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention – MICCAI 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: Researchers experimented on three medical imaging datasets: PAPILA for fundus image classification, HAM10000 for skin cancer classification, and MIMIC-CXR for chest X-ray classification.
Data Details: 364 were chosen from PAPILA, 1062 from HAM10000 and 1062 from MIMIC-CXR.
Topic: We introduce CALIN, an inference-time calibration method designed to mitigate the associated biases by estimating the amount of calibration needed.
Notes: We consider 5 different metrics in the experiments. (i) classification accuracy, (ii) expected calibration error for quantifying the reliability of predicted confidence, (iii) mean equalized odd ratio between sex and age (EOR) for fairness evaluation, (iv) confidence calibration error gap (CCEG) for calibration fairness evaluation, and (v) equity-scaling measure of calibration error (ESCE) for accessing the overall calibration performance adjusted by subgroups’ performance.
Debias Details: CALIN operates through a bi-level calibration procedure, effectively mitigating unfairness. Experimental results on three medical imaging datasets demonstrate that CALIN not only enhances fairness but also improves overall predictive performance and exhibits minimum fairnessutility trade-off.

Title: Elucidating Mechanisms of Demographic Bias in LLMs for Healthcare
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2025
Data Source: NA
Data Details: They use a dataset introduced by another study. This other study used real doctor's annotations and patient summaries from the MIMIC-IV-Note dataset as well as examples of hallucinations from LLM generated output. They developed a framework to generate annotations and patient summaries with reduced hallucations.
Topic: Researchers apply methods in mechanistic interpretability to healthcare LLMs.
Notes: They used activation patching and showed that they were able to affect the frequency at which patient vignettes of certain genders or races were generated.
Debias Details: Researchers applied activation patching at different layers and looked for differences in LLM-generated patient vignettes.

Title: Navigating the Rabbit Hole: Emergent Biases in LLM-Generated Attack Narratives Targeting Mental Health Groups
Data Source: RabbitHole Dataset
Data Details:
This dataset "a large corpus of 190, 599 generative outputs from the Mistral 7B LLM, seeded with mildly
positive and negative stereotypes and recursively prompted for increased toxicity".
Topic: Investigating stigmas against mental health in LLMs by seeing how close mental health is to negative concepts in network analysis and community detection. Researchers also test if mental health gets brought up in negative scenarios, even when unprompted.
Notes: Researchers conduct a centrality analysis of the RabbitHole network that aims to "capture entity transitions across generative chains". They use the Leiden algorithm to analyze this network, and they use a generative chain of prompts to make text more toxic.
Disability Bias:
Yes(people with panic disorder, people with dysgraphia, people
with bipolar disorder, people with dyscalculia, people with
dyslexia, ...)

Title: Disaggregated Health Data in LLMs: Evaluating Data Equity in the Context of Asian American Representation
Journal: Vol. 8 No. 1 (2025): Proceedings of the Eighth AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society (AIES-25) - Main Track I
Data Source: None
Data Details: Researchers prompted GPT-3.5 model to return important health issues that one of a certain background should be concerned with. Each data point is an LLM response to the prompt “Generate important health issues that I should be concerned about as a [demographic group]. Be faithful to all the provided demographic features I did not specify.”
Topic: Researchers apply statistical and machine learning tools to assess whether LLMs deliver appropriately disaggregated and equitable information within the larger category of Asian Americans.
Notes: Researchers compute Kendall-Tau Rank Correlation Coefficient and the Kendall-Tau Rank Distance within their embedding space, constructed by BERTopic API.
Racial Bias: Yes(Bangladeshi, Bhutanese, Burmese, Cambodian, Chinese, Filipino, Hmong, Indian, Indonesian, Japanese, Korean, Laotian, Malaysian, Mongolian, Nepalese, Okinawan, Pakistani, Sri Lankan, Taiwanese, Thai, Vietnamese)

Title: Building Trust in Clinical LLMs: Bias Analysis and Dataset Transparency
Journal: Proceedings of the 2025 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
Data Source: Semantic Scholar Open Research Corpus (S2ORC) API, PubMed Central FTP service, OpenAlex database
Data Details: Each data point, or what the paper calls data sample, can come in the form of an abstract, paper, or wiki-page. They made sure to apply deduplication.
Topic: Researchers developed HC4: Healthcare Comprehensive Commons Corpus, a curated pretraining dataset of more than 89 billion tokens.
Notes: Researchers used the BOLD dataset and bias evaluation framework. They used Wikipedia scraped text samples along with demographic variables of race and gender as input and classified sentiment for the LLM output. They also measured things like Net Presription Bias.
Racial Bias: Yes(african american,asian american,european american,hispanic or latino americans)
Debias Details: Researchers compared bias evaluations of LLMs trained on the study's original dataset with the corresponding bias evaluations of other datasets like SlimPajama and FineWeb.

Title: Unmasking and Quantifying Racial Bias of Large Language Models in Medical Report Generation
Data Source: Researchers use PMC-Patients, a large collection of 167,000 patient summaries extracted from case reports in in PubMed Central, as the source to generate synthetic patient profiles. Note that they did not include the original data in their experimentation, as they used AI to remove demographic data and pre treatment data.
Data Details: Each data point is a patient summary from case reports in in PubMed Central with the pre-treatment details and demographic details removed. Instead, one of four hypothetical race labels were added. Originally, researchers extracted a large collection of 167,000 patient summaries extracted from case reports in in PubMed Central, but they narrowed it down heavily.
Topic: Quantitative analysis of racial bias in medical report generation
Notes: There was a focus on racial bias measurement, using pair-wise win rate to compare two racial groups at once. Notably, the model favored white patients with superior and immediate treatments,  longer hospitalization stays, and better recovery outcomes, which is also reflected in the higher projected cost.

Title: Uncovering Judgment Biases in Emergency Triage: A Public Health Approach Based on Large Language Models
Journal: Proceedings of the 4th Machine Learning for Health Symposium, PMLR 259, pages 420-439, 2025
Data Source: Researchers collected reports from approximately 480,000 entries to the Adult ED of the Bordeaux University Hospital.
Data Details: Each data point is a report containing the exact moment of the admission, the sex of the patient, the chief complaint, history of present illness, past medical history, an array of vital signs (heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, among others), and the associated triage score.
Topic: Judgment bias in emergency triage decision-making by LLMs
Notes: Researchers used typical data science accuracy measures for classification tasks like accuracy score, F1, recall, and precision.

Title: ChatGPT for Mental Health Applications: A study on biases.
Journal: AIMLSystems '23: Proceedings of the Third International Conference on AI-ML Systems
Data Source: Reddit based datasets: CLPsych 2019, Dreaddit, Suicide Risk Dataset
Data Details: Mental health scenarios and counseling prompts
Topic: Researchers study biases in mental health applications of ChatGPT
Notes: They want to focus on suicide prevention and understand which groups are more protected or at risk when interacting with ChatGPT3.5 Turbo.

Title: Africa Health Check: Probing Cultural Bias in Medical LLMs
Journal: Proceedings of the 2025 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: PubMed Corpus, then reviewed by researchers.
Data Details: The multiple choice questions that they ultimately generated fell into three variants: No-context questions simply ask for the most appropriate answer without providing patient details, location, or cultural information. Full-Context questions do provide this information. Misleading-Context questions are similar to Full-Context questions, but they contain deceptive cultural cues.
Topic: Researchers study how culturally adapted LLMs are in considering traditional medicine, in particular traditional African herbal medicine. Medical LLMs are normally biased to allopathic, or Western, treatment options.
Notes: The unique contribution of this paper is the Cultural Bias Attribution metric, which quantifies how input prompts drive culturally biased responses. They also create their own dataset through a systematic review of PubMed and they prompt PubMed with this. They use ChatGPT-4o as part of the MCQ generation pipeline.
Debias Details: Different inference methods, like Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, and Instruction-Tuning were compared along the metrics of cultural bias attribution and cultural bias score.

Title: Towards conversational assistants for health applications: using ChatGPT to generate conversations about heart failure
Journal: Proceedings of the 26th Annual Meeting of the Special Interest Group on Discourse and Dialogue
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: The data is separated into several folders, aave, domain, reason-prompt, reasoning, and sdoh. Within these folders are files containing conversations between patients and healthcare specialists. Each data point is a simulated conversation between a patient and a healthcare professional.
Topic: Researchers test the ability of prominent commercial LLMs to generate patient-health educator dialogues while incorporating African American Vernacular English and also Social Determinants of Health.
Notes: They concluded that despite the different prompting methods improving output quality, "ChatGPT still lacks the empathy and engagement" necessary for effective communication in healthcare. They also included human reviewers, who rated randomly selected generated conversations. The reviewers read 3 conversations for each prompting method and completed a Likert scale style questionnaire. They also used metrics like round adherence rate, follow-up rate, ratio of words, and format adherence rate.
Debias Details: Researchers used prompting methods described as: aspects, African American Vernacular English, Social Determinants of Health (SDOH), and SDOH-informed reasoning.

Title: EMPEC: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models Across Diverse Healthcare Professions
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Open Source
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple choice question from "officially published past exams for healthcare professionals in the Professional and Technical Examinations of Taiwan, Republic of China". These questions were written to assess medical knowledge and clinical skills for a diverse array of healthcare professions. Note that they used ChatGPT and other software to translate the dataset into other languages.
Topic: Researchers introduce a new dataset called Examinations of Medical PErsonnel in Chinese (EMPEC), which they use to evaluate the knowledge and performance of LLMs across underrepresented healthcare professions like Optometrists and Audiologists.
Notes: ChatGPT-4 had the highest performance in 19 out of the 20 medical professions tested, but Qwen1.5-70B-Chat achieves highest accuracy in the Traditional Chinese Medicine Practitioner domain. Models trained in English benefit from an English translation. Despite using different tokens, Traditional and Simplified Chinese have similar performance, improving the accuracy of some models while decreasing for others.

Title: Polish-English medical knowledge transfer: A new benchmark and results
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source:
Researches scrape past exams given to Polish physicians and dentists from 2008 to 2024, categorized by specialization. This includes the  LEK (Lekarski Egzamin Koncowy, Medical ´
Final Examination), LDEK (Lekarsko-Dentystyczny Egzamin
Koncowy, Dental Final Examination), and the PES (Panstwowy Egzamin Specjalizacyjny, National Specialization Examination). Both the LEK and LDEK were translated into English.
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple choice question from one of the exams described in the data source field: LEK, LDEK, PES.
Topic: Researchers tested the ability of LLMs to answer medical examination questions from various Polish exams. Many questions were translated into English as well, and the results between different models, types of questions, and language of questions.
Notes: Results were assessed in terms of accuracy on the exam questions. The study comapred percentages of correct answers across different exams and languages. Model performance was also compared to average human performance. Note that English translations of the original material in Polish was provided by human experts. A key takeaway from the study's observations is that language transfer could be more effective as general model performance scales up.

Title: Evaluating Large Language Models on Health-Related Claims Across Arabic Dialects
Journal: Proceedings of the 1st Workshop on NLP for Languages Using Arabic Script
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Researchers used the AraFacts dataset, which contains natural claims which have been fact-checked.
Data Details: Each data point is a question create through a question generation pipeline, where natural claims fact-checked by a fact-checked website like Fatabyyano or Misbar are feed into ChatGPT and created into question-answer pairs in different dialects of Arabic.
Topic: Researchers measure how LLMs react to health claims written in different Arabic dialects. They want to see how well subtle cultural and linguistic details are accounted for, and how models can be accurate and culturally sensitive in their response.
Notes: Researchers also used a metric called the cultural sensitivity analyzer, which measured how well models adapted their responses to different dialects.
Language Bias: Yes(Saudi Arabic, Egyptian Arabic, Lebanese Arabic, Moroccan Arabic)

Title: Towards Algorithmic Fidelity: Mental Health Representation across Demographics in Synthetic vs. Human-generated Data
Journal: Proceedings of the 2024 Joint International Conference on Computational Linguistics, Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC-COLING 2024)
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: Another study, called asked patients to describe the largest source of stress in their life and how they dealt with that stress. They used this human generated data, and then they augmented the data set with ChatGPT-3.
Topic: Researchers prompt LLMs to provide significant significant stressors in people's lives depending on their demographic identities. They then conduct pairwise analysis of how often certain issues are associated with certain demographic groups.
Notes: They found that existing stereotypes were reinforced. Women were often stressed about health, news and social media, news and politics, family, and relationships. Men were more concerned about finances and unemployment, racism, and police brutality. Along racial lines, African Americans more associated with bio, health, body, percept, racism, and police brutality. Asians more associated with perfectionism, comparing to others, work, school, and reward. Hispanics more concerned with immigration, family, social interactions, etc. Whites more associated with general stress, news and social media, relationships, and uncertainty.

Title: What Goes In, Must Come Out: Generative Artificial Intelligence Does Not Present Algorithmic Bias Across Race and Gender in Medical Residency Specialties
Data Source: American Academy of Medical Colleges (AAMC) list of medical professions.
Data Details: Researchers found a list of medical professions from the AAMC website and selected 19.
Topic: Researchers study representation of medical professionals across diverse specialties in the image generating model DALL-E2.
Notes: They found that OpenAI's DALL-E2's predictions generally aligned with the current demographic landscape of medical residents. This suggests that the output was mirroring the training data rather than projecting algorithmic bias into the output.
Racial Bias: Yes(Asian, Black or African American, Hispanic or Latino or of Spanish origin, white)

Title: A vignette-based evaluation of ChatGPT's ability to provide appropriate and equitable medical advice across care contexts.
Data Source: Origional data,w written by reviewer: 96 advice-seeking text vignettes created by physicians, presented to ChatGPT96 advice-seeking text vignettes created by physicians, presented to ChatGPT
Data Details: Each data point was a vignette representing a scenarios in preventive care, acute care, or palliative care. Three clinical scenarios were created by four practicing physicians in emergency, critical care, and palliative medicine.
Topic: Researcher evaluated (1) the clinical appropriateness of ChatGPT’s responses to advice-seeking questions across the clinical spectrum… and (2) the equity of the responses by evaluating whether they differ by patient race, gender, or insurance status.
Notes: The original ChatGPT based on GPT-3.5 was used… ChatGPT responses were collected between February 2 and February 10 2023
Other Bias: Yes (insurance status (good insurance vs no insurance))

Title: Assessing the diagnostic performance of ChatGPT in dermatology across Fitzpatrick phototypes and skin of color.
Journal: American Academy of Dermatology (J Am Acad Dermatol)
Data Source: 170 dermatology clinical images (85 non–skin of color, 85 skin of color) of 17 common skin diseases; labels confirmed by 3 board‑certified dermatologists
Data Details: Each data point is an image that may consist of 17 common dermatoses; 5 non‑SoC + 5 SoC images per disease; Fitzpatrick V–VI or non‑Caucasian for SoC; images matched on variant, severity, location
Topic: Diagnostic performance and fairness of ChatGPT‑4o across Fitzpatrick phototypes / skin of color vs non‑SoC dermatology images
Notes: Overall accuracy high, but markedly worse on skin of color (SoC ≈ 71.8% vs non‑SoC ≈ 90.6%); underrepresentation of SoC images in training likely cause; authors urge cautious clinical use and more diverse datasets
Other Bias: Yes (skin phototype / skin of color vs non‑SoC；also mentions “health care disparities, skin of color” as key words)

Title: Assessment of Medically Relevant Ageism Inherent in Large Language Models.
Data Source: Multiple-choice question dataset about medically related ageism scenarios (constructed text prompts, English + Japanese)
Data Details: Multiple-choice questions on ageism in healthcare, 403 labeled questions across five ageism dimensions, English and Japanese versions of each question
Topic: Assessment of medically relevant ageism in general-purpose LLMs using a labeled multiple-choice dataset in English and Japanese.
Notes: Many LLMs exhibit stereotypical ageism, Almost all LLMs tended to be biased in the direction of stereotypes, Many of the models exhibited an anti-stereotypical tendency in relation to the neglect of the medical complaints.

Title: Evaluating the impact of sex bias on AI models in musculoskeletal ultrasound of joint recess distension.
Data Source: 5,000 de-identified MSKUS knee ultrasound images from community clinics in Ontario, Canada
Data Details: Each data point is an image. It consists of de-identified knee MSKUS images, 2010–2018, adult patients, suprapatellar view, 4 cohorts: male/female × with/without distension, expert sonographer labeled presence/absence
Topic: Sex bias effects on CNN performance for knee synovial recess distension (and sex classification) from ultrasound
Notes: Female-only and balanced training outperform male-only; sex classifier 95% accuracy; Grad-CAM shows sex-specific focus areas

Title: Exploring biases related to the use of large language models in a multilingual depression corpus.
Data Source: RADAR-MDD speech recordings (English, Spanish, Dutch) + PHQ‑8, from London / Amsterdam / Barcelona
Data Details: RADAR-MDD free-response speech task, PHQ‑8 <10 vs ≥10 (low vs high severity), Whisper-large-v3 ASR transcripts, downsampled to 208 samples per condition for balance
Topic: demographic (age, gender) and language biases in LLM-based depression severity classification on multilingual speech-derived text
Notes: age balance had stronger impact than gender; performance varies across languages; tests multiple LLMs
Other Bias: Yes (broader sociodemographic fairness: ethnicity, education, SES; also ASR bias)
Evaluated LLMs: FlanT5, RoBERTa, mBERT (MentalBERT), GPT‑2, BERT (multilingual), LLaMA, MentaLLaMA

Title: Large language models propagate race-based medicine.
Data Source: LLM outputs from ChatGPT-3.5, GPT‑4, Bard, Claude on 9 race-based-medicine questions（45 responses each）
Data Details: 9 race-related medical questions, 4 commercial LLMs, 5 runs per question per model, 45 outputs per model total
Topic: whether major LLMs regurgitate race-based medicine and racist medical tropes
Notes: all four models had runs with race-based medicine; stochastic; some fabricated equations
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT (3.5 versions May 12, Aug 3), GPT‑4, Bard (May 18, Aug 3), Claude (May 15, Aug 3)

Title: Sociodemographic Bias in Large Language Model-Assisted Gastroenterology.
Data Source: 100 synthetic gastroenterology clinic vignettes generated by ChatGPT‑o1, each with 34 sociodemographic versions; LLM outputs to 4 clinical questions
Data Details: Each data point is a combination of the following. They select one of 100 ChatGPT‑o1 generated GI cases. They select one type of demographic information, that being either the control or 33 other sociodemographic versions (race, gender identity, SES, housing, etc.). Then, they select one of 4 clinical questions. Each version was given to 10 LLMs.
Topic: sociodemographic bias (race, gender identity, income, housing, etc.) in LLM recommendations for GI cases, esp. mental health referrals
Notes: 10 LLM ensemble; 136k responses; higher odds of mental health referral for transgender, unhoused, low-income, unemployed vignettes
Racial Bias: Yes (Asian, Black, Hispanic, Native American, White)
Other Bias: Yes (low income, middle income, high income, unemployed, unhoused)
LGBTQ+ Bias: Yes (transgender man, transgender woman, nonbinary (they/them), gay/lesbian, bisexual, heterosexual)

Title: Uncovering Language Disparity of ChatGPT on Retinal Vascular Disease Classification: Cross-Sectional Study.
Journal: Journal of Medical Internet Research (J Med Internet Res)
Data Source: 1226 Chinese FFA reports + corresponding clinical diagnoses from one hospital (SAHZU Eye Center)
Data Details: 1226 FFA reports, 728 patients, single center (SAHZU), 2016–2021, labels: normal vs 6 retinal vascular diseases
Topic: language disparity (Chinese vs English prompts) in ChatGPT’s retinal vascular disease diagnosis and reasoning on Chinese FFA reports
Notes: direct English prompts best F1=80.05%; Chinese prompts ~6–7% lower; Step vs Direct trade-offs; reasoning eval (hallucinations, misinformation, incompleteness)

Title: Quality safety and disparity of an AI chatbot in managing chronic diseases: simulated patient experiments.
Data Source: Simulated patient (SP) scripts for unstable angina and asthma + ERNIE Bot / ChatGPT‑4o / DeepSeek‑R1 responses
Data Details: 64 factorial scenarios (6 binary traits) × 2 diseases × 3 repeats = 384 SP–AI trials
Topic: 384 ERNIE Bot sessions + 40 ChatGPT‑4o + 40 DeepSeek‑R1quality, safety, and sociodemographic disparity in AI chatbot management of chronic diseases via simulated patients
Notes: ERNIE: diagnostic accuracy 77.3%, correct medication 94.3%, unnecessary tests 91.9%, unnecessary meds 57.8%; more intensive care for older/wealthier SPs; ChatGPT‑4o / DeepSeek R1 even higher accuracy but more overprescribing
Other Bias: Yes (household economic status (poor vs rich), health insurance (UEMI vs URRMI), hukou (urban vs non‑urban) and residence (urban vs rural) tested but no effect)

Title: Beyond Tokens: Fair Evaluation of French Large Language Models for Clinical Named Entity Recognition.
Journal: Volume 316: Digital Health and Informatics Innovations for Sustainable Health Care Systems
Data Source: CANTEMIST‑FR subset of FRASIMED: 1301 synthetic French oncological reports with morphology entities
Data Language: French (CANTEMIST‑FR); Spanish used only as source for projection
Data Details: 1301 French synthetic oncology notes, 15,978 morphology entities, created by cross-lingual annotation projection from Spanish CANTEMIST
Topic: clinical French NER for oncology morphologies; comparison of token‑ vs entity‑level metrics; BERT vs ChatGPT prompting
Notes: CamemBERT best token‑level F1, FlauBERT best entity‑level; DrBERT many PARTIALs; ChatGPT has low recall but decent relaxed precision
Evaluated LLMs: CamemBERT (Base), FlauBERT (Base), CamemBERT‑Bio (Base), DrBERT (Base), ChatGPT (GPT‑3.5, zero‑shot and few‑shot)

Title: Beyond the Rubric: Cultural Misalignment in LLM Benchmarks for Sexual and Reproductive Health.
Journal: Proceedings of the 5th Workshop on Evaluation and Comparison of NLP Systems
Data Source: Subset of HealthBench conversations filtered for SRH (637 queries, 330 single‑turn used), Responses from Myna Bolo SRH chatbot (RAG + intent detection, WhatsApp)
Data Details: Each data point is a question related to sexual and reproductive health. Of the 637 questions, 330 where single‑turn used; 244 were answered by Myna Bolo and auto‑graded.
Number of Prompts: 244 chatbot responses auto‑graded; plus GPT‑4 used once per HB conversation to classify SRH vs non‑SRH
Topic: Researchers studied cultural misalignment and Western bias in HealthBench’s automated rubric when evaluating SRH chatbot for Global South (India)
Notes: HealthBench rubric often assumes US laws, diet, insurance; penalizes India‑specific, concise, patient‑centric answers judged safe by local experts; authors propose culturally adaptive, region‑aware evaluation layers
Other Bias: Yes (cultural, regional, socioeconomic bias in benchmark assumptions)
Debias Details: Concept: keep HealthBench backbone; add region‑tagged criteria, property‑based checks, human‑centered (patient) perspective

Title: Demographic Bias of Expert-Level Vision-Language Foundation Models in Medical Imaging.
Data Source: Experiment on Existing ModelsFive public chest x‑ray datasets: MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert, NIH ChestX-ray14, PadChest, VinDr
Data Details: Each data point may come from Chest x‑ray datasets, number of images / patients, demographics (sex, age, race), some with radiologist annotations; resized and normalized before evaluation. It comes from the following: MIMIC 357,167; CheXpert 223,458; NIH 112,120; PadChest 160,736; VinDr 5,323 images.
Topic: Bias and fairness of chest x‑ray vision‑language foundation models vs radiologists
Notes: Model underdiagnoses marginalized and intersectional groups; encodes demographic info strongly
Evaluated LLMs: CheXzero, Knowledge-enhanced Auto Diagnosis (KAD), supervised DenseNet baseline
Debias Details: Modify prompts by adding demographic attributes to try to reduce fairness gaps; mixed results

Title: Accuracy of Spanish and English-generated ChatGPT responses to commonly asked patient questions about labor epidurals: a survey-based study among bilingual  obstetric anesthesia experts.
Data Source: Patient-oriented questions about labor epidurals (10 common questions), translated to Spanish and English, used as prompts to ChatGPT (GPT-3.5)
Data Details: 10 commonly asked labor epidural questions were entered into ChatGPT (version 3.5) in English and Spanish, generating 20 model responses. These responses were evaluated via a Qualtrics survey by 10 bilingual obstetric anesthesiologists, of whom 7 responded. Each expert rated each ChatGPT-generated response using a 5-point Likert scale. A single data point corresponds to one expert’s rating of one ChatGPT-generated response.
Size: The data comprised 139 answers, with 70 Spanish and 69 English responses
Topic: Comparison of accuracy of ChatGPT-generated answers to patient questions about labor epidurals in English vs Spanish, as judged by bilingual obstetric anesthesiologists.

Title: Bias Perpetuates Bias: ChatGPT Learns Gender Inequities in Academic Surgery Promotions.
Data Source: Prompts to ChatGPT to generate letters of recommendation for academic surgery promotion candidates (gender-specifically “male” vs “female”)
Data Details: 6 recommendation letters generated (3 for a hypothetical female surgeon, 3 for a hypothetical male surgeon) using ChatGPT with identical qualification prompts but differing only by specified gender.
Topic: Evaluating whether gender identity included in prompts to ChatGPT leads to differences in language, length, and descriptors used in academic surgery promotion letters, reflecting real-world bias.
Notes: The main focus is implicit gender bias in AI-generated professional recommendation letters

Title: Detecting Sociodemographic Biases in the Content and Quality of Large Language Model-Generated Nursing Care: Cross-Sectional Simulation Study.
Data Source: LLM-generated nursing care plans created by prompting GPT-4 with clinical vignettes that vary only in patient sociodemographic attributes
Data Details: Standardized clinical vignettes were constructed representing 96 unique sociodemographic profiles (varying sex, age, income, education, residence). GPT-4 was prompted to generate 9600 care plans for these profiles. A stratified subsample of 500 plans was later quality-reviewed by nursing experts
Size: 9600 generated care plans total; 500 reviewed by experts
Topic: Whether large language model–generated nursing care plans differ systematically in content themes and clinical quality based on patient sociodemographic characteristics (sex, age, income, education, urban/rural residence)
Notes: Care plans were evaluated both quantitatively (theme frequency, text length) and qualitatively (nursing expert quality ratings)

Title: Disparities in medical recommendations from AI-based chatbots across different countries/regions.
Data Source: Standardized clinical questions based on previously published endometrial cancer case reports used as prompts for three AI chatbots
Data Details: 6 endometrial cancer patient cases were adapted from previously published case reports into standardized clinical vignettes and structured prompts. Each vignette was entered once into three AI chatbots (Bing, Bard, and ChatGPT-3.5) from four different geographic locations (Indonesia, Nigeria, Taiwan, USA) within a 24-hour window. This resulted in 72 chatbot-generated medical recommendations. Responses were coded and randomized before being evaluated in a double-blind manner by ten gynecologic oncologists using five parameters (relevance, clarity, depth, focus, and coherence) on a 5-point Likert scale. A single data point corresponds to one chatbot-generated medical recommendation.
Size: 72 (6 vignettes × 3 chatbots × 4 locations each asked once)
Topic: Investigation of geographic disparities in the quality of medical recommendations provided by AI-based chatbots for the same clinical questions when accessed from different countries/regions
Other Bias: Yes (geographic disparity: Indonesia, Nigeria, Taiwan, United States)

Title: Evaluating ChatGPT's Multilingual Performance in Clinical Nutrition Advice Using Synthetic Medical Text: Insights from Central Asia.
Data Source: Synthetic medical text: 50 mock patient profiles used to generate dietary recommendations in three languages (English, Russian, Kazakh) via ChatGPT-4
Data Details: 50 mock patient profiles covering dietary scenarios; identical nutritional prompts were provided in English, Russian, and Kazakh to ChatGPT-4, producing advice later evaluated on several criteria
Size: 50 generated responses per language → total 150 outputs
Topic: Assessment of ChatGPT-4’s ability to provide personalized, consistent, and practical clinical nutrition advice across English, Russian, and Kazakh

Title: Evaluation and Bias Analysis of Large Language Models in Generating Synthetic Electronic Health Records: Comparative Study.
Data Source: Synthetic electronic health records (EHRs) generated by prompting LLMs with standardized EHR-template prompts across 20 diseases
Data Details: Standardized prompt templates were used to generate synthetic EHRs across 20 diseases. Multiple LLMs were evaluated for record completeness and demographic representativeness. Bias was assessed using statistical parity difference metrics.
Topic: Comparative evaluation of large language models for synthetic EHR generation, with emphasis on data quality and demographic bias.
Racial Bias: Yes (race categories reported in the study, e.g., White, Black, Hispanic, Asian)

Title: Gender Bias in Artificial Intelligence-Written Letters of Reference.
Data Source: Letters of reference (LORs) generated by prompting ChatGPT with standardized applicant descriptions differing only by gender
Data Details: Ten discrete hypothetical candidate profiles were constructed using combinations of school, research topic, and extracurricular activities. Each profile was converted into two prompts differing only by candidate name and pronouns (Emily/she and Michael/he). The 20 prompts were entered into ChatGPT (version 3.5) in two separate sessions with alternating prompt order, generating 40 letters of recommendation. Address and signature sections were removed prior to analysis. The letters were analyzed using an open-source gender bias calculator to quantify the proportion of male- versus female-associated words. A single data point corresponds to one ChatGPT-generated letter of recommendation.
