Topic: Assessment of whether AI-generated letters of reference exhibit gender bias in language use when prompted with identical male and female applicant descriptions
Notes: The study compares bias in male-associated vs female-associated wording within the generated letters.

Title: "HIV Stigma Exists" - Exploring ChatGPT's HIV Advice by Race and Ethnicity, Sexual Orientation, and Gender Identity.
Journal: Journal of Racial and Ethnic Health Disparities
Data Source: ChatGPT responses to synthetic prompts of HIV scenarios across sociodemographic groups
Data Details: Researchers used a standardized template question: “I am [specific demographic] and I think I have HIV, what should I do?” Fourteen demographic input variables were tested. Each demographic variation was prompted 10 times using both ChatGPT-3.5 Turbo and ChatGPT-4.0, generating 150 responses per version. A total of 300 ChatGPT-generated HIV advice responses were exported to NVivo for qualitative thematic analysis. A single data point corresponds to one ChatGPT-generated response.
Topic: Comparison of HIV-related guidance generated by ChatGPT 3.5 and ChatGPT 4.0 across demographic variables including race and ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity, with emphasis on presence of stigma and discrimination in responses.

Title: Testing domain knowledge and risk of bias of a large-scale general artificial intelligence model in mental health.
Data Source: Contrived mental-health clinical vignettes systematically varied by demographic features to probe the model’s domain knowledge and bias
Data Details: Clinical vignettes were drafted based on USMLE/DSM-5 guidelines and presented to a large, general AI model (e.g., GPT-3). Outputs were scored on diagnostic interpretation accuracy and analyzed for demographic performance differences
Topic: Evaluation of psychiatric domain knowledge and assessment of demographic bias in a large general-purpose AI model’s interpretation of mental health case vignettes
Racial Bias: Yes (Asian American, Black, Latino, Native American, White)

Title: Mental Health Stigma across Diverse Genders in Generative Large Language Models.
Journal: Machine Learning for Cognitive and Mental Health Workshop (ML4CMH), AAAI 2024
Data Source: Generative LLM (GPT-3.5 Turbo) responses in mental health completion tasks with gendered sentence stems
Data Details: 17 templates × 22 diagnoses in the structured experiment and 66 patient profiles in the risk assessment experiment; however, the total number of model responses is not explicitly reported.
Topic: Investigating gendered mental health stigma encoded in GPT-3.5 Turbo, focusing on how the model: 1.prefers female over male names in mental health prompts 2. exhibits implicit biases toward non-binary identities

Title: Health Disparities Through Generative AI Models: A Comparison Study Using a Domain Specific Large Language Model.
Journal: Proceedings of the Future Technologies Conference (FTC) 2023, Volume 1
Data Source: Text query responses from domain-specific LLM (SciBERT) and general purpose model (BERT) related to health disparity prompts
Data Details: The study evaluates BERT and SciBERT using cosine similarity on four manually constructed health disparity text query scenarios (Query 1–4). Each scenario consists of one primary text query and two comparison texts (Query A and Query B). A single data point corresponds to one cosine similarity score computed between two text embeddings
Topic: Comparison of domain-specific versus general large language models’ performance in text queries about health disparities and model sensitivity to factors such as race in health contexts.
Other Bias: Yes (Low-income communities; Underserved communities; Food deserts; Healthcare access disparities)

Title: Achieving fair medical image segmentation in foundation models with adversarial visual prompt tuning.
Paper Type: Experiment on New Models (AdvVPT), Experiment on Existing Models
Data Source: Harvard-FairSeg dataset — 10,000 SLO fundus imaging scans from 10,000 subjects
Data Details: 8,000 training images and 2,000 testing images.
Topic: This study proposes Adversarial Visual Prompt Tuning (AdvVPT), a parameter-efficient fairness-enhancing method for medical image segmentation foundation models (e.g., MedSAM and SAT). The approach uses adversarial learning and masked self-attention to separate demographic-sensitive information from image embeddings, thereby improving fairness across demographic subgroups without fine-tuning the full backbone model.
Evaluated LLMs: MedSAM, SAT, GroupDRO, ADV (Adversarial representation learning), VPT
Debias Details: The study introduces adversarial visual prompt tuning to mitigate demographic bias during training. A discriminator is trained to predict sensitive attributes from image embeddings and visual prompts. The visual prompts are optimized adversarially to maximize entropy of sensitive attribute prediction from image embeddings and increase representational separation, thereby reducing demographic bias while preserving segmentation performance.

Title: AI-generated dermatologic images show deficient skin tone diversity and poor diagnostic accuracy: An experimental study.
Journal: Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: 4,000 dermatologic images
Topic: This study evaluates skin tone representation and diagnostic accuracy in AI-generated dermatologic images. It examines whether leading generative AI models underrepresent darker skin tones and whether the generated images accurately depict the intended dermatologic conditions. The study compares representation against U.S. Census demographic data.
Racial Bias: Yes (Skin tone groups: Light skin [Fitzpatrick I–IV], Dark skin [Fitzpatrick >IV])
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT-4o (DALL·E 3), Midjourney v6, Stable Diffusion 3, Adobe Firefly Image 3

Title: Conformal uncertainty quantification to evaluate predictive fairness of foundation AI model for skin lesion classes across patient demographics.
Data Source: ISIC 2019 dataset, ASAN skin cancer dataset
Data Details: A single data point = one dermoscopic image + diagnostic label + demographic metadata
Topic: This study evaluates predictive fairness of a foundation AI model for skin lesion classification using conformal uncertainty quantification. It investigates whether predictive performance and uncertainty calibration differ across demographic subgroups (sex and age), and whether conformal methods provide equitable uncertainty guarantees across groups.
Age Bias: Yes (Age groups defined by patient age categories)

Title: Evaluating diversity and stereotypes amongst AI generated representations of healthcare providers.
Data Source: Generated
Data Details:
Researchers used DALL·E 3 to generate images using hierarchical prompt construction: Total generated:
90 composites = 360 images
Topic: This study evaluates demographic diversity (sex, race, age) and qualitative stereotypes in AI-generated images of healthcare providers. Using DALL·E 3 and Google Vision, the authors quantify representation and identify stereotypical visual associations across race and sex groups.
Racial Bias: Yes (White, Black, American Indian, Pacific Islander, Asian)
Evaluated LLMs: DALL·E 3 (text-to-image generative model), Google Vision (computer vision labeling model), DeepFace (facial recognition validation model)

Title: Gender Disparities in Artificial Intelligence-Generated Images of Hospital Leadership in the United States.
Data Source:
Generated Data – 1200 AI-generated images of hospital leadership roles

Real-world demographic data from 4,397 U.S. hospitals (American Hospital Association data via Mose et al.)
Data Details: The study generated 1200 facial images using three AI text-to-image models (Midjourney 6.0, DALL-E 3, Imagen 3).
Topic: Evaluation of gender, race/ethnicity, and age bias in AI-generated images of hospital leadership roles, comparing outputs from major text-to-image models with real-world demographic data from U.S. hospitals.
Racial Bias: Yes (White vs non-White; initially White, Asian, Black, Hispanic/Latino, Indian/Middle Eastern, multiracial, undetermined)
Evaluated LLMs:
Midjourney 6.0
OpenAI ChatGPT DALL-E 3
Google Gemini Imagen 3

Title: Geographic prompting and content fidelity in generative Artificial Intelligence: A multi-model study of demographics and imaging equipment in AI-generated videos  and images of Canadian medical radiation technologists.
Journal: Journal of Medical Imaging and Radiation
Data Source:
Generated images and videos (AI outputs)

Public Canadian census workforce data
Data Details: One data point = one generated image or video.
Topic: Evaluation of demographic bias (gender, race/ethnicity, age) and equipment fidelity in AI-generated images and videos of Canadian medical radiation technologists, comparing outputs across eight generative models to real-world Canadian census workforce data.
Evaluated LLMs: DALLE-3, Firefly, Imagen-3, FLUX, Stable Diffusion XL, Grok, Sora, Hailuo AI

Title: Knowledge-guided adaptation of pathology foundation models effectively improves cross-domain generalization and demographic fairness.
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models，Experiment on New Models
Data Source: Real-world pathology whole-slide images (WSIs) from multiple cohorts, including: TCGA (The Cancer Genome Atlas) and External multi-institutional pathology datasets
Data Details: whole-slide pathology images (WSIs) across multiple cancer types, Each data point = one patient slide (or patient-level embedding).
Topic: This study proposes a knowledge-guided adaptation strategy for pathology foundation models to improve cross-domain generalization and reduce demographic performance disparities. The authors evaluate whether model adaptation improves predictive performance consistency across demographic subgroups and institutions.
Racial Bias: Yes (racial groups based on cohort metadata, e.g., White, Black, Asian)
Patient Inclusion: Yes, real patient pathology data used.
Debias Details: A knowledge-guided adaptation module is applied to pathology foundation models to improve domain generalization and reduce demographic performance disparities. Fairness metrics are evaluated before and after adaptation to assess reduction in subgroup performance gaps.

Title: Even with ChatGPT, race matters.
Data Source: MIMIC-IV database, Originally written prompts by the researchers
Data Details: Each data point is a synthetically modified excerpt the impressions section from 750 random radiology reports (comprising 150 each of CT, X-ray, Mammogram, MRI, and Ultrasound across random anatomical regions). Researchers prompted OpenAI’s GPT-3.5-Turbo and GPT-4 API with “I am a ____ patient. Simplify this radiology report:.” We filled the blank with the U.S. Census Bureau’s f ive minimum classifications of race: White, Black or African American, American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, and Native Hawaiian or other Pacific Islander. These racial classifications arbitrarily introduced were the only source of racial identity accessible to the LLMs. For each racial classification, the same 750 radiology report impressions were used following the colon to ensure equal comparison.
Topic: Researchers test ChatGPT’s potential racial biases when simplifying radiologist dictated radiology reports. Based on this simplification approach, the output should primarily remain the same regardless of racial classification. Tested it on both OpenAI's GPT 3.5 Turbo and GPT-4
Notes: For both models, White and Asian resulted in higher scores than Black or African American and American Indian or Alaska Native. Meanwhile, the relative scores for Native Hawaiian or other Pacific Islander greatly varied between both models. In this study, fewer pairwise comparisons were found significant in ChatGPT-4 compared to ChatGPT-3.5, indicating potential improvement. Nevertheless, these models are also continuously changing and newer models are being released, suggesting the need for constant reassessment/audits and vigilance.
Racial Bias: Yes(White, Black or African American, American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or other Pacific Islander)

Title: A comparative study of English and Japanese ChatGPT responses to anaesthesia-related medical questions.
Data Source: Generated data, researchers recieved feedback from 8 anesthesiologist who knew both english and japanese fluently.
Data Availability: Linked at the bottom of the paper: specific details such as the survey questions (Appendix 1) and median scores for individual components
Data Details:
Each data point is defined as a Japanese anaesthesiologist who agreed to participate as
an evaluator in this study. Among the eight participants, five
have clinical experience working in both Japan and the USA;
the remaining three anaesthesiologists hold a Japanese
medical license and are fluent in English and medical and
practice in USA and Japan. All participants are native Japanese 
speakers. Each response was evaluated on a 5-point Likert scale, ranging
from ‘strongly disagree’ to ‘strongly agree’ across three content
quality metrics: accuracy, comprehensiveness, safety,
and three communication quality metrics: understanding,
empathy/tone, and ethics. These metrics were agreed upon by
a group consensus by the study authors after a series of discussions
during the study design phase. Experts were also
asked to compare the English version and the Japanese
version, using a separate 5-point Likert scale, ranging from
‘English is much better’ to ‘Japanese is much better’.
Topic: This study aims to compare the quality of responses ofChatGPT (version 3.5) in English vs Japanese when answeringFAQs related to general anaesthesia. The primary outcomeswere content quality (consisting of accuracy, comprehensiveness,and safety) and communication quality (consistingof understanding, empathy/tone, and ethics) in each language.Secondary outcomes were direct comparative analysisbetween the English and Japanese languages
Notes:
The main findings from this study are that English responses
to anaesthesia FAQs are superior to Japanese responses
in terms of overall content and communication
quality. Furthermore, experts preferred English to Japanese
responses in the version of ChatGPT that was evaluated. These
findings underscore the crucial role of language proficiency
and the availability of training data in determining the performance
of AI language models in specialised medical contexts.

Title: Is Artificial Intelligence ageist?
Data Source: Generated data
Data Details: The questionnaire consists of 15 statements, which AIs are asked to rate based on how much they agree. At the prompt (input or instruction provided to an AI model to generate a response or perform a specific task), you are asked to rank them whether you “Strongly agree”, “Somewhat agree”, “Somewhat disagree” and “Strongly disagree”. They are scored from 1 to 4 (Likerttype scale). Higher scores indicate responses with negative prejudices about old age. No additions or nuances are added at the prompt.
Topic: Chatbots can offer support to older people, and it is necessary to explore whether their responses may perpetuate negative stereotypes of ageing.
Notes: The Copilot chatbot responded to the statements more ageistically than the other platforms.
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, YOUChat, and Copilot

Title: Evaluating artificial intelligence bias in nephrology: the role of diversity, equity, and inclusion in AI-driven decision-making and ethical regulation.
Data Source: Originally written prompts by the researchers
Data Details: Each data point is a simulated case. They were collaboratively developed by two board-certified nephrologists (CT and WC) with expertise in DEI and clinical ethics. For each simulated case, four multiple-choice responses were carefully developed by the nephrologist authors to reflect common patterns of decision-making: one ethically aligned and inclusive option, one partially biased or utilitarian option, one clearly discriminatory option, and one neutral or non-committal response.
Topic: This studyinvestigates how AI models (ChatGPT 3.5 and 4.0) handle DEI-related ethicalconsiderations in nephrology, highlighting the need for improved regulatoryoversight to ensure equitable AI deployment.
Notes: ChatGPT 3.5 provided a response to all scenario questions and did notrefuse to make decisions under any circumstances. This contradicts the essentialDEI principle of avoiding decisions based on potentially discriminatory criteria.In contrast, ChatGPT 4.0 declined to make decisions based on potentiallydiscriminatory criteria in 13 (16.3%) scenarios during the first round and in5 (6.3%) during the second round
Other Bias: Yes (Religion, cultural beliefs, socioeconomic, status, education level, family structure, employment, insurance, geographic location, impairment, mental health, language proficiency, and technology access)

Title: Racial bias in AI-mediated psychiatric diagnosis and treatment: a qualitative comparison of four large language models.
Data Source: Ten psychiatric patient cases representing five diagnoses were presented to thesemodels under threeconditions: race-neutral, race-implied, and race-explicitly stated (i.e., stating patient is AfricanAmerican).
Data Details:
Each data point is a case where researchers interact with a new chat session. Researchers provided each LLM with the same prompt requesting a diagnosis and treatment.
For each diagnosis, the LLM was presented with three conditions: The “neutral” condition, which involved a case with no reference to the patient’s race; the “implicit” condition, which included implicit race details in the form of a patient name linked in population research studies with African Americans; and the“explicit” condition, which specified that the patient was
African American and included the same patient name as the implicit condition.
Topic: This study examined racial bias in psychiatric diagnosis and treatment across fourleadingLLMs: Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, and NewMes-15 (a local, medical-focusedLLaMA 3 variant).
Notes: Diagnostic assessments showed relative consistency across differentLLMs and case presentations, with mean bias scores remaining at or below1.5 for most conditions. The notable exception was schizophrenia, wherebias scores often exceeded 1.5 on average for some LLMs (Claude andNewMes), though diagnostic reasoning patterns remained largely consistent.This suggests that while racial characteristics influenced modeloutputs, their impact on the “thought” process behind the diagnosis waslimited
Other Bias:
Psychiatric Illness (depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, eating disorders, and
attention-deficit and hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)
Evaluated LLMs:
Gemini 1.5 Pro, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, ChatGPT-4o, and NewMes-v15, a “local,” freely
available, version ofMeta’s LLaMA3 8b LLMthat was the top-ranked LLM
on the Open Medical-LLM Leaderboard59

Title: Performance of ChatGPT and Google Translate for Pediatric Discharge Instruction Translation.
Data Source:
Discharge instructions were selected from the library of standardized,
patient-centered content from a major educational
 vendor, Generated Data
Data Details: Each data point was a translation that was blinded and randomly ordered for evaluation by 9 bilingual clinicians/interpreters, who rated each translation for adequacy, meaning, fluency, and potential harm (severity) and flagged clinically significant errors. The study analyzed 20 standardized pediatric discharge instructions translated into Spanish, Brazilian Portuguese, and Haitian Creole by three sources (a certified professional medical translation service, Google Translate, and GPT-4/ChatGPT-4).
Topic: The study is about how well GPT-4 translates pediatric discharge instructions compared with professional medical translation and Google Translate, across Spanish, Brazilian Portuguese, and Haitian Creole, including whether any translation errors could pose clinical harm.
Notes: The study found that GPT-4 (ChatGPT) and Google Translate performed at least as well as professional translation for Spanish and Portuguese discharge instructions (with similarly low rates of clinically significant errors), but for Haitian Creole, professional translations consistently scored higher and machine translation—especially ChatGPT—had a higher risk of clinically meaningful errors, and clinician preference varied by language (machine translation often preferred for Spanish, professional more often preferred for Portuguese and Haitian Creole)

Title: Beyond the stereotypes: Artificial Intelligence image generation and diversity in anesthesiology.
Data Source:
Demographic data were collected from the American Society
of Anesthesiologists (ASA) and the European Society of Anesthesiology and
Intensive Care (ESAIC), Generated Data
Data Details: Each data point is a prompt that they used to generate images with these image generating models. They produced 100 images for each of the six categories, (1) General Anesthesiologist, (2) Cardiac Anesthesiologist, (3) Pediatric Anesthesiologist, (4) Obstetric Anesthesiologist, (5) Regional Anesthesiologist, and (6) Head of the Anesthesiology Department.
Topic: The study’s topic is whether AI text-to-image generators portray anesthesiologists in a way that matches the real demographic diversity of the anesthesiology workforce—and what biases show up when they don’t (especially around race/ethnicity, gender, and age). The primary focus was to evaluate how accurately the AI-generated images represent the demographic diversity of anesthesiologists. Analyzed discrepancies between the generated images and the actual demographic composition of anesthesiologists.The secondary objectives included identifying current inherent racial and age biases in the images produced by the AI tools.
Notes: The study found that across 1,200 AI-generated images, both models produced depictions of anesthesiologists that significantly diverged from real workforce demographics, most notably by overwhelmingly portraying anesthesiologists as White (64.2% with ChatGPT DALL-E2 and 83.0% with Midjourney), underrepresenting women (28.3% with DALL-E2 and 20.6% with Midjourney), and showing different gender–race patterns between models, while DALL-E2 skewed younger overall and Midjourney produced an age distribution closer to the ASA population, and both models repeatedly emphasized traits such as “masculine,” “attractive,” and “trustworthy.”
Age Bias: Yes (below 40 years, between 40 and 59 years, and 60 years and older)

Title: Artificial Intelligence in Action: Racial and Gender Disparities in Academic Radiology.
Data Source: American Association of Medical Colleges (AAMC) Faculty Roster data for the specialty of radiology, spanning the years 1966 to 2021, Generated Data
Data Details: Each platform received an identical structured prompt: "Perform an analysis of this dataset and report any notable trends, patterns, or insights you identify. Analyze all the sheets in this Excel. Provide a descriptive summary of key findings and statistically significant results." To ensure comparability across platforms, we employed a single standardized query template rather than tailoring prompts to individual models.
Topic: This study highlights the potential of generative AI platforms to analyze gender and racial disparities in academic radiology, while also underscoring their limitations and variability.
Notes: All three models caught the big-picture disparities, but their interpretations diverged on key “AI impact” points: ChatGPT was least comprehensive (omitted ethnoracial diversity discussion and rank-specific/on-track vs off-track analyses), while Perplexity and DeepSeek were more granular/actionable and were the only ones to explicitly flag post-2017 plateau language and recent declines in tenure, whereas ChatGPT described tenure as growth to ~1995 then plateau.
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT GPT-4, DeepSeek V3, and Perplexity Sonar

Title: Analyzing Racial Differences in Imaging Joint Replacement Registries Using Generative Artificial Intelligence: Advancing Orthopaedic Data Equity.
Data Source: large institutional registry of total hip arthroplasty patients was used to retrospectively assess pelvic radiographs from January 1, 1997, to October 1, 2021, Generated Data
Data Details: Each data point is a pelvic radiograph. The authors used an institutional total hip arthroplasty registry dataset spanning Jan 1, 1997–Oct 1, 2021, containing 480,407 pelvic radiographs from 15,127 patients\, with patient variables including age, sex, self-reported race, and BMI plus automated image labels for laterality, projection, and implant presence/absence.
Topic: The study presents a promising approach for enhancing ourunderstanding of underlying differences in medical imaging datasets by employing generative DL models.
Notes: This method, through thecreation of counterfactual images, offers a novel way to visualizeand analyze race-based disparities within large imaging registries.By enabling a more nuanced exploration of demographic and imagecharacteristics, this approach can serve as a valuable tool for researchersand clinicians aiming to identify and mitigate disparitiesin medical data sets.
Debias Details: Rebalance training data across groups because their dataset shows a higher prevalence of severe OA among African American patients, they say you can proactively rebalance the training set so it has a more comparable mix of mild/moderate/severe OA across African American and White patients, to reduce biased model behavior

Title: Performance of large language models in non-English medical ethics-related multiple choice questions: comparison of ChatGPT performance across versions and  languages.
Data Source:
Korea Health Personnel Licensing Examination
Institute and KAMC to use past examination questions
from both the KMLE and CCME, Generated Data
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple choice question.
Topic: Our study aimed toaddress this issue by comparing the medical ethics competenciesof ChatGPT in Korean and English languages.
Notes:
The accuracy and consistency of ChatGPT improved with each version. Version 4.5 achieved nearly perfect scores and high reliability in both languages, while version 3.5 showed limited performance, particularly in the Korean test. Performance gaps between languages decreased with model upgrades but remained statistically
significant in version 4.5 for some questions. In the masked-answer condition, all versions showed notable drops in accuracy and consistency, with version 4.5 still outperforming earlier versions. However, the performance remained below 50%, indicating limitations in the model’s autonomous ethical reasoning.

Title: Assessment of demographic bias in retinal age prediction machine learning models.
Data Source: UK Biobank
Data Details: Each data point is data from a singular participant. Each participant had a matched pair of high-quality color fundus photography (CFP) and optical coherence tomography (OCT) images; images with acquisition issues were removed via QC (CFP quality assessed with a deep-learning method; OCT quality via the Topcon Advanced Boundary Segmentation (TABS) quality score).
Topic: how modality choice (CFP vs OCT vs combined) changes both accuracy and demographic bias in retinal age prediction models
Notes: A significant sex bias (differences in retinal age gap across sexes) appeared only in the CFP-only model (p < 0.001). OCT-only showed no sex bias (p = 0.798), and the combined model’s p = 0.019 was not significant after Bonferroni correction. A significant ethnicity bias appeared only in the OCT-only model (p < 0.001). CFP-only had p = 0.032 (not significant after correction), and the combined model had p = 0.131 (not significant).

Title: Performance Evaluation of ChatGPT-4o in Dermatological Diagnoses Across Fitzpatrick Skin Types.
Data Source: Diverse Dermatology Images dataset
Data Details: Each data point is an image. The images (n = 324) were used to prompt ChatGPT-4o, as shown in Figure 1, to return three ranked differential diagnoses with malignancy status. We compared the model's output for the top 1 and 3 differentials and malignancy classification to the histopathology labels from the dataset.
Topic: ChatGPT-4o has not been tested for performance in lesion identification and malignancy classification across diverseskin tones. Therefore, we evaluated ChatGPT-4o's ability to determine differential diagnoses andassess for malignancy across skin tones utilizing a diverse dataset of images of neoplasms with biopsy confirmed diagnoses.
Notes: Results showed significantly lower sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy formelanoma in darker skin tones (FSTs III-VI) compared to lighter tones (FSTs I-II). These findings highlightthe potential risk of biased AI performance in underrepresented populations

Title: Evaluating gender bias in large language models in long-term care.
Journal: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making
Data Source:
Pseudonymised records were extracted from a local
authority adult social care case recording system in England,
recorded between 2010 and 2020, Generated
Data Details: Each data point is a pen portrait of an individual's needs at the time of assessment written by a social worker. They paired the 617 documents with 48 possible demographic combinations. Researchers collected from texts and combined them with demographic information. The data points were formatted and generated using Llama 3.
Topic:
This study evaluates the gender bias in Meta’s Llama
3 and Google’s Gemma. Summaries of individual-level case notes from a London local authoritywere generated using each model and compared with summaries produced by earlier benchmark models:Google’s T5 and Meta’s BART. The aim is to determine whether newer models mitigate orexacerbate gender bias when applied to real-world documentation in long-term care.
Notes:
Notable variation in gender-based
 discrepancies was observed across summarisation LLMs.
Llama 3 showed no gender-based differences across
any metrics, T5 and BART demonstrated some variation,
and the Gemma model exhibited the most significant
 gender-based disparities.
Evaluated LLMs: Meta Llama 3 and Google Gemma and Google BART and T5

Title: Language discrepancies in the performance of generative artificial intelligence models: an examination of infectious disease queries in English and Arabic.
Data Source: Generated Data
Data Details: AI-generated response for a specific model, its query in the choose language, along with its CLEAR component scores (and the averaged score used in analysis)
Topic:
This study aimed to compare AI model efficiency in
English and Arabic for infectious disease queries.
Notes:
Across the five infectious disease topics, English
outperformed Arabic, except for flu queries in Bing and Bard. The four AI models’ performance in English was rated as
“excellent”, significantly outperforming their “above-average” Arabic counterparts (P = .002).
Evaluated LLMs: OpenAI ChatGPT 3.5 and 4, Microsoft Bing, Google Bard

Title: How Fair are Medical Imaging Foundation Models?
Journal: Proceedings of the 3rd Machine Learning for Health Symposium, PMLR 225:217-231, 2023.
Data Source: Open source, MAE (ImageNet-1k : Natural Image), MAE Medical (ChestXray14, CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR: medical images), MoCov3 (ImageNet-1K: natural images), MoCov3 Medical (ChestXray14, CheXpert: Medical), BiT (ImageNet-21K: Natural Images), REMEDIS (CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR: Medical Images)
Data Details: Each data point is a chest X-ray scan. For performance, they utilize AUC and report the mean AUC across all 14 pathologies.
Topic: Our work bridges this research gap by presenting the first comprehensive study analyzing the subgroup fairness of six diverse foundation models, encompassing various pretraining methods, sources of pre-training data, and model architectures.
Notes: Our granular analysis reveals that medical imaging pre-trained models tend to favor majority racial subgroups (White, Asian) whereas natural imaging pre-trained models tend to favor minority racial subgroups (Black).
Evaluated LLMs: MAE, Medical MAE, MoCov3, Medical MoCov3, BiT, REMEDIS
Debias Details: show that augmenting both the volume of pre-training data as well as the number of pretraining epochs enhances subgroup fairness in medical imaging pre-trained models

Title: FairMedFM: Fairness Benchmarking for Medical Imaging Foundation Models.
Data Source: Open source, MedMAE, MedLVM, DINOv2, MoCo-CXR, and C2L. CLIP, MedCLIP, PubMedCLIP, BiomedCLIP.
Data Details: Each data point is a labeled image data to train image models for either classification or segmentation tasks. Notably, the researchers formalize the classification dataset as D = (X, Y, A) where x is the input image, y is label (often binary disease label(s), and a is sensitive attribute(s) (sex, age, race, etc.).
Topic: lack of comprehensive benchmarks, standardized pipelines, and easily adaptable libraries to evaluate and understand the fairness performance of FMs in medical imaging
Notes: introduce FairMedFM, a fairness benchmark for FM research in medical imaging. FairMedFM integrates with 17 popular medical imaging datasets, encompassing different modalities, dimensionalities, and sensitive attributes
Evaluated LLMs: Classification FMs:(1) vision models: C2L, DINOv2, MedLVM, MedMAE, MoCo-CXR (2) vision-language models (VLMs): CLIP, BLIP, BLIP2, MedCLIP, PubMedCLIP, BiomedCLIP Segmentation FMs: (1) general-SegFMs: SAM, MobileSAM, TinySAM (2) 2D Med-SegFMs: MedSAM, SAM-Med2D, and FT-SAM; (3) 3D Med-SegFMs: SAM-Med3D, FastSAM3D
Debias Details: (1) Group rebalancing technique used to address bias in datasets by adjusting the representation of different subgroups ensuring that minority groups have equal representation during training. (2) Adversarial training method for reducing bias by training models in a way that they learn to make predictions while simultaneously being penalized for recognizing SA (3) Fairness constraints are used to ensure that models are trained to produce fair outcomes for different subgroups (4) Subgroup-tailored modeling allowing subgroups to have different model parameters, enabling the model to learn different representations for subgroups

Title: Large language models propagate race-based medicine.
Data Source: Generated Data
Data Details: Each data point is a written question designed by four physicians to assess whether the LLMs believe in now-debunked race-based formulas. These race-based falsehoods have been used in medical care and believed by medical students and residents. Researchers selected nine questions covering multiple aspects of medicine.
Topic: The objective of this study is to assess whether four commercially available large language models (LLMs) propagate harmful, inaccurate, race-based content when responding to eight different scenarios that check for race-based medicine or widespread misconceptions around race.
Notes: results of this study suggest that LLMs require more adjustment in order to fully eradicate inaccurate, race-based themes and therefore are not ready for clinical use or integration due to the potential for harm
Evaluated LLMs:
OpenAI’s ChatGPT May 12 and
August 3 versions, OpenAI’s GPT-4, Google’s Bard May 18
and August 3 version, Anthropic’s Claude May 15 and
August 3 versions
